title: Ming Foundation Profile Internal Review Round 1 Results

results.reviewer_role: Ming Foundation Architecture and Ethics Review

dimensions.finding: Participation levels, decision inputs, impact minimums, conflict rules, and required records are clearly separated.
P0–P5 may be misread as a maturity ladder; the profile needs explicit purpose-specific, non-hierarchical, supported-decision, refusal, and non-retaliation rules.
Impact minimums and temporary-exclusion limits support bounded, reversible decisions.
Notice, correction, revocation, conflict, and appeal routes are structurally visible.
P0 requires stronger independent authorization, shortest-duration, support, and later-notice safeguards.
Communication support is named but not operationalized for literacy, disability, language, neurodivergence, or trauma-sensitive participation.
The profile lacks a scenario set demonstrating how the same person may hold different levels for different purposes and how disagreement changes a decision.
Age, capacity, representation, and safeguarding implications require affected-person, domain, and jurisdiction review.

results.reviewer_role: Ming Foundation Architecture and Ethics Review

dimensions.finding: Representative types, evidence levels, impact mapping, authority fields, failure states, and review questions are explicit.
The profile needs a subject-accessible challenge route, supported decision-making preference, and treatment of informal caregivers without converting care into authority.
E1–E4 may incentivize overcollection; a least-evidence and no-document-when-not-necessary rule is required.
Authority evidence needs purpose separation, deletion or expiry propagation, and restrictions against reuse as identity, immigration, financial, or behavior evidence.
Conflict handling needs stronger operator-as-risk, coercion, family violence, and protected-source scenarios.
Verification and challenge routes need language, disability, low-literacy, and remote-access alternatives.
The evidence levels and failure-state vocabulary are implementable and machine-testable.
The meaning and sufficiency of authority evidence require jurisdiction-specific legal and institutional review.

results.reviewer_role: Ming Foundation Architecture and Ethics Review

dimensions.finding: Profile classes, completion states, backup handling, exception controls, and review questions are distinct.
Affected people need a plain-language view of what remains, why it remains, who can use it, and how to challenge an exception.
The default review intervals must be more visibly separated from permission to retain and must require earlier deletion when purpose ends.
Primary, derived, indexed, shared, backup, and exception states provide strong rights traceability.
The profile needs explicit balancing for safety evidence, reporter protection, false labels, and restoration from backup.
The state model can support a comprehensible status display once translated into plain language.
Completion and backup states are sufficiently concrete for implementation testing.
Retention periods, legal holds, safeguarding duties, and deletion exceptions require jurisdiction and domain review.

results.reviewer_role: Ming Foundation Architecture and Ethics Review

dimensions.finding: Service classes, target evidence, freshness classes, resource records, and stale behavior are coherently separated.
S0 and S3 prevent false monitoring or handoff claims and preserve informed user choice.
F0–F3 intervals should become risk- and context-sensitive maximum review defaults rather than appear universally sufficient.
The profile does not require unnecessary personal case disclosure to verify public resources.
S2 needs minimum staffing, failed-handoff, overload, outage, cross-region, and after-hours evidence before an active-handoff claim.
Resource and service verification needs disability access, language, age appropriateness, cost, eligibility, and safe-contact fields.
Service classes, verification dates, expiry, owner, and failure states are testable.
Emergency, safeguarding, professional, and local-resource claims require jurisdiction and local-domain review.
